name: Eta HTTP warm-loop performance vs Go net/http

description: Baseline: parametrize perf_compare with ETA_PERF_{ITERS,WARMUP,TIMEOUT_MS} env vars; add autoresearch.{sh,md}.

asi.hypothesis: establish baseline numbers for the loop with reduced iter counts (15 iters, 3 warmup, 500ms timeout) so wall-clock stays under ~30s per iteration
asi.h2_baseline: timeouts on every kept-alive request (2 of 4 scenarios) — counted as 500ms each in the metric
asi.go_reference: h1=0.022, h2_get_1k=0.022, h2_get_1m=0.442, h2_post_1m=1.914 ms — host is healthy
asi.first_wins_to_chase: (1) eliminate the H1 ~44ms floor — search for literal delays / sleeps / synchronous flushes in eta-http/{client,h1}, (2) get H2 kept-alive reuse working — likely a multiplexer / stream_state / window-update bug after the first request
asi.env_vars_for_diagnostic_runs: ETA_PERF_ITERS, ETA_PERF_WARMUP, ETA_PERF_TIMEOUT_MS already wired into run.ml
asi.next_action_hint: read autoresearch.md, then start with: ETA_PERF_ITERS=3 ETA_PERF_TIMEOUT_MS=2000 ./autoresearch.sh to capture the actual H2 timeout signature with full backtraces; instrument run.ml or eta-http with phase timings if 44ms H1 root cause is not obvious from the source

description: fix: coalesce H1 request writes into single send() to eliminate Nagle + delayed ACK stall (47ms → 1ms)

asi.hypothesis: Coalesce H1 request writes into a single send() to avoid Nagle + TCP delayed ACK interaction. The multi-write pattern (one write per header line via Eio.Flow.copy_string) created multiple small TCP segments. On reused connections, the server delayed ACK for 40ms (TCP_DELACK_MIN), causing the read_response_head to stall.
asi.root_cause: Nagle + delayed ACK: write_to_flow sent ~10 small writes (method, space, path, version, each header line, blank line). On a reused connection with no unacked data yet, first write sends immediately, subsequent writes get Nagled waiting for ACK, server delays ACK up to 40ms. First request was fast because the TCP connection setup ACK cleared the Nagle condition.
asi.fix: Buffer the entire request (headers + body) into a single Buffer and send with one write_string call. This ensures the entire HTTP request is a single TCP segment, eliminating the Nagle/delayed-ACK interaction.
asi.h2_still_broken: true — both nginx h2 tls GET scenarios still timeout at 500ms cap
asi.go_stable: h1=0.019, h2_get_1k=0.023, h2_get_1m=0.454, h2_post_1m=1.968 — host healthy
asi.next_action: Investigate H2 kept-alive reuse bug. The h2 GET scenarios timeout on every request after warmup. Suspect multiplexer/stream_state/window_update issue in packages/eta-http/h2/.

description: fix: close H2 request body for GET/empty-body requests + set TCP_NODELAY on all connections (H2 reuse now works, 1006ms → 11.5ms total)

asi.h2_body_close_fix: For GET/Empty body requests, `opened.request_body` (H2.Body.Writer.t) was never closed. In HTTP/2 this means END_STREAM is never sent, so the server waits forever. POST worked because `h2_write_fixed_body_sync` explicitly calls `H2.Body.Writer.close`. Fixed by closing the body writer before `wait_for_response` for Empty and Fixed [] bodies.
asi.tcp_nodelay_fix: Set TCP_NODELAY on all TCP connections via `Eio_unix.Resource.fd_opt` + `Unix.setsockopt TCP_NODELAY true`. This eliminates Nagle + delayed-ACK stalls on both H1 and H2.
asi.h1_get_1k: 1.05ms vs Go 0.021ms — 50×, still room but good enough for milestone
asi.hypothesis: Two independent fixes: (1) H2 GET requests were missing H2.Body.Writer.close, causing END_STREAM to never be sent on reused connections. (2) TCP_NODELAY was off, causing ~40ms Nagle+delayed-ACK stalls on H1 and H2.
asi.next_action: The h2_get_1m (4.4ms vs Go 0.5ms) and h2_post_1m (6.0ms vs Go 2.1ms) are the remaining gaps. Suspect body-reading path in H2 multiplexer or chunked/stream decoding. Also h1_get_1k is 50× Go — investigate if H1 pool/channel overhead can be reduced.
